past_medical_history: Atrial fibrillation, diagnosed 5 years ago, on warfarin but self-discontinued 3 weeks ago due to concerns about bruising
Hypertension, diagnosed 15 years ago, poorly controlled
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 10 years ago
Congestive heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (LVEF 40%), diagnosed 2 years ago
Chronic kidney disease stage 3a (baseline creatinine 1.4 mg/dL)

medications.indication: Heart failure and atrial fibrillation rate control

family_history: Mother had a stroke at age 62 and died
Father had hypertension and type 2 diabetes, died of heart failure at age 70
Sister with atrial fibrillation and hypertension

social_history.living_situation: Lives alone in a first-floor apartment; daughter lives nearby and checks on her daily
social_history.exercise: Very limited due to exertional dyspnea and knee pain

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert but appears frightened and confused. Oriented to self and place but not time. Expressive aphasia: speaks in short telegraphic phrases with frequent paraphasic errors. Able to follow simple one-step commands. Naming impaired (2/6 objects). Repetition impaired. Reading comprehension partially preserved. Unable to write.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, reactive to light and accommodation. Conjugate right gaze deviation present; can cross midline with oculocephalic maneuver. Right homonymous hemianopia on confrontation testing. Right lower facial droop, forehead sparing. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue protrudes midline.
neurological_exam.motor: Right upper extremity: 1/5 deltoid, 0/5 wrist extension, 0/5 grip. Right lower extremity: 2/5 hip flexion, 1/5 ankle dorsiflexion. Left upper and lower extremities: 5/5 throughout. Right arm pronator drift with arm falling immediately to bed.
neurological_exam.sensory: Diminished pinprick and light touch sensation over the right face, arm, and leg. Extinction to double simultaneous stimulation on the right. Proprioception impaired in the right hand and foot.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Left upper extremity 2+, left lower extremity 2+. Right upper extremity 3+, right lower extremity 3+ with extensor plantar response (positive Babinski sign) on the right. Left plantar response is flexor.
neurological_exam.coordination: Unable to test on right side due to severe weakness. Left finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin normal.
neurological_exam.gait: Unable to ambulate due to right hemiparesis.
neurological_exam.additional: NIHSS score: 16. Right-sided neglect present on line bisection task. No nuchal rigidity. No involuntary movements.

patient.chief_complaint: Sudden onset right-sided weakness and difficulty speaking
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Pradeep Singh is a 55-year-old right-handed African American woman with a history of atrial fibrillation (previously on warfarin, self-discontinued 3 weeks ago), hypertension, type 2 diabetes, heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, and chronic kidney disease who was brought to the emergency department by his daughter after the acute onset of right-sided weakness and speech difficulties. The daughter reports that he spoke to his mother on the phone at approximately 09:00 this morning and he sounded completely normal, discussing plans for a church event this weekend. The daughter stopped by to drop off groceries at 10:45 and found his mother sitting on the kitchen floor, leaning against the refrigerator, with his right arm limp and speech garbled. The patient was able to say 'help' and 'arm' but could not form complete sentences. He appeared to have been attempting to reach the phone. There was a broken coffee mug on the floor beside her. The daughter immediately called 911. EMS arrived at 11:02 and documented right facial droop, right upper and lower extremity weakness, and expressive aphasia. A prehospital stroke alert was activated. Field glucose was 142 mg/dL. The patient was transported to our comprehensive stroke center, arriving at 11:28. On the cardiac monitor he was found to be in atrial fibrillation with a rate of 108 bpm. He was mildly hypertensive at 192/104 mmHg. He was anxious, tearful, and attempting to speak but producing only fragments and paraphasic errors. When asked why he stopped his warfarin, his daughter explained that his mother had noticed large bruises on his arms and legs over the past month and decided on his own to stop the medication without consulting his physician. There is no history of recent head trauma, seizure, fever, or new headache. His last known well time is established at 09:00, approximately 2 hours and 28 minutes before ED arrival.

findings.location: Left middle cerebral artery territory involving the left frontal lobe (Broca's area), left precentral gyrus, left parietal cortex, and left insular cortex
findings.size: Approximately 4.8 x 3.5 cm in maximal axial dimensions

signal_characteristics.DWI: Markedly hyperintense signal consistent with restricted diffusion
signal_characteristics.ADC: Corresponding hypointensity confirming true restricted diffusion
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Developing hyperintensity suggesting onset within the past 2-4 hours

findings.mass_effect: Mild sulcal effacement over the left frontoparietal convexity. No midline shift. Mild compression of the left lateral ventricle frontal horn.
findings.borders: Margins conform to the left MCA superior division vascular territory

signal_characteristics.MRA: Abrupt cutoff of the left MCA at the proximal M1 segment with absent flow distal to the occlusion
signal_characteristics.TOF: Loss of flow-related enhancement in the left MCA distribution

additional_observations: Right MCA, bilateral ACA, and bilateral PCA patent on MRA
mri.impression: Acute left MCA territory infarction with restricted diffusion involving the left frontal lobe including Broca's area, precentral gyrus, parietal cortex, and insular cortex. No hemorrhagic transformation. Chronic small vessel ischemic disease and old right basal ganglia lacunar infarct. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria
T-wave inversions in leads V4-V6 consistent with LVH with strain pattern
No acute ST elevation or depression suggesting acute coronary syndrome
ecg.interpretation: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response at 108 bpm. Left ventricular hypertrophy with strain pattern. No acute ischemic changes.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Hematocrit 35.7 % (L), Chloride 97 mEq/L (L), BUN 29 mg/dL (H), Creatinine 1.4 mg/dL (H), Glucose 165 mg/dL (H), INR 1.2  (H), Total Cholesterol 203 mg/dL (H), LDL 139 mg/dL (H), Triglycerides 174 mg/dL (H), Troponin I 0.08 ng/mL (H), BNP 502 pg/mL (H), HbA1c 8.4 % (H).

findings.location: Left MCA at the proximal M1 segment
findings.description: CTA: Complete occlusion of the left MCA at the proximal M1 segment. No filling of the M2 branches. Left ICA terminus is patent.

findings.location: Bilateral carotid bifurcations and vertebrobasilar system
findings.description: CTA: Mixed calcified and soft plaque at bilateral carotid bifurcations. Left ICA approximately 30% stenosis. Right ICA approximately 25% stenosis. Mild vertebrobasilar atherosclerotic changes.

additional_observations: Bilateral vertebral arteries patent with normal caliber
Basilar artery patent without significant stenosis
Circle of Willis demonstrates bilateral A1 segments and intact anterior communicating artery
output.impression: Mild bilateral carotid bifurcation atherosclerotic disease without hemodynamically significant stenosis. No cervical vessel dissection. Clinical correlation recommended.

chambers.Left Atrial Appendage: Spontaneous echo contrast (smoke) seen in left atrium; LAA not well visualized on TTE
chambers.Right Ventricle: Mildly dilated with mildly reduced function

output.impression: Abnormal values: Left Ventricular Ejection Fraction 38%  (H), Left Atrial Volume Index 42 mL/m2 (H), Left Ventricular Wall Thickness Concentric LVH with global hypokinesis  (H), Mitral Valve Moderate mitral regurgitation  (H), Left Atrial Appendage Spontaneous echo contrast (smoke) seen in left atrium; LAA not well visualized on TTE  (H), Right Ventricle Mildly dilated with mildly reduced function  (H), Pericardium Trace pericardial effusion  (H).

events.description: Persistent atrial fibrillation throughout the 24-hour recording period
No episodes of sinus rhythm identified
Occasional periods of rapid ventricular response (>110 bpm) primarily during daytime hours
Frequent premature ventricular complexes (218 in 24 hours, approximately 0.2% burden)
One 3-beat run of non-sustained ventricular tachycardia at rate 142 bpm

findings: Persistent atrial fibrillation throughout the 24-hour recording period
No episodes of sinus rhythm identified
Occasional periods of rapid ventricular response (>110 bpm) primarily during daytime hours
Frequent premature ventricular complexes (218 in 24 hours, approximately 0.2% burden)
One 3-beat run of non-sustained ventricular tachycardia at rate 142 bpm
output.impression: Twenty-four hour Holter monitoring demonstrates persistent atrial fibrillation with suboptimal rate control (average 92 bpm with episodes of RVR). Brief non-sustained VT noted, which may be related to underlying cardiomyopathy. No significant pauses.

findings.region: Left internal carotid artery at the bifurcation
findings.signal: Peak_systolic_velocity: 110 cm/s; End_diastolic_velocity: 32 cm/s; ICA_CCA_ratio: 1.6; Estimated_stenosis: Less than 50% (NASCET criteria)

findings.region: Right internal carotid artery at the bifurcation
findings.signal: Peak_systolic_velocity: 98 cm/s; End_diastolic_velocity: 28 cm/s; ICA_CCA_ratio: 1.4; Estimated_stenosis: Less than 50% (NASCET criteria)

output.impression: Mild (<50%) stenosis of bilateral internal carotid arteries. Patent bilateral vertebral arteries. The degree of carotid stenosis is mild and unlikely to be the primary stroke mechanism. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.size: Core infarct volume approximately 22 mL; penumbral tissue approximately 78 mL; mismatch ratio 4.5
findings.description: CBF: Severely reduced in left MCA territory core region; CBV: Reduced in core, relatively preserved in penumbra; MTT: Prolonged throughout the left MCA territory; Tmax: Tmax >6s volume approximately 100 mL; Tmax >10s volume approximately 28 mL

output.impression: CT perfusion demonstrates a highly favorable target mismatch profile with a small ischemic core (22 mL) and substantial salvageable penumbral tissue (78 mL). Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: timing of anticoagulation initiation after acute cardioembolic ischemic stroke atrial fibrillation

results.title: 2019 AHA/ASA Guidelines for the Early Management of Patients With Acute Ischemic Stroke
results.authors: Powers WJ, Rabinstein AA, et al.
results.key_finding: For patients with AF-related ischemic stroke, initiation of oral anticoagulation between 4 and 14 days after stroke onset is reasonable depending on infarct size and hemorrhagic transformation risk.

results.title: ELAN Trial: Early versus Late Anticoagulation in Stroke with AF
results.key_finding: Early initiation of DOACs (within 48h for minor stroke, within 6-7 days for moderate stroke) was non-inferior to later initiation in preventing recurrent stroke or systemic embolism.

results.title: DOAC Selection in CKD: Apixaban vs Rivaroxaban
results.key_finding: Apixaban is preferred in patients with CKD stage 3-4 due to lower renal clearance (25%) compared to rivaroxaban (33%) or dabigatran (80%).

output.summary: Current evidence supports initiating oral anticoagulation with a direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC) within 4-14 days after acute cardioembolic stroke, with timing guided by infarct size and hemorrhagic transformation risk. The ELAN trial supports earlier initiation for small-to-moderate infarcts. In patients with CKD, apixaban is preferred due to its lower renal clearance. DOACs are generally preferred over warfarin for non-valvular AF-related stroke given their favorable safety and efficacy profile.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke, left middle cerebral artery territory, cardioembolic etiology secondary to atrial fibrillation

differential.key_distinguishing: No hemorrhage on CT/MRI; DWI restriction in a vascular territory with MRA showing M1 occlusion confirms ischemic etiology
No witnessed seizure, no epilepsy history, imaging confirms acute infarction with large vessel occlusion
Age 68 without migraine history; persistent deficits with MRI-confirmed infarction excludes this diagnosis
Prehospital glucose 142 mg/dL and ED glucose 162 mg/dL; hypoglycemia excluded
MRI shows DWI restriction in vascular territory without enhancing mass; pattern inconsistent with tumor

optimal_actions.action: Activate stroke code and perform rapid assessment including NIHSS, last known well time, and fingerstick glucose
optimal_actions.expected_finding: NIHSS 16, LKW approximately 2.5 hours prior, glucose 142 mg/dL

optimal_actions.action: Obtain emergent non-contrast CT head to exclude intracranial hemorrhage
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No hemorrhage; possible subtle early ischemic changes in left MCA territory

optimal_actions.action: Obtain stat labs including CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, troponin, and glucose
optimal_actions.expected_finding: INR 1.1 confirming patient is not anticoagulated; glucose elevated; CKD at baseline
Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response at 108 bpm

optimal_actions.action: Administer IV alteplase 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg) with 10% bolus and remainder over 60 minutes, given patient is within the 4.5-hour window and INR is not elevated
Obtain CTA head and neck to evaluate for large vessel occlusion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left MCA proximal M1 occlusion confirmed

optimal_actions.action: Consult neurointerventional surgery for emergent mechanical thrombectomy given large vessel occlusion with favorable perfusion profile
Obtain brain MRI with DWI to confirm and characterize infarct extent
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Acute left MCA territory infarction with restricted diffusion

optimal_actions.action: Manage blood pressure permissively (target <185/110 pre-tPA, then <180/105 for 24h post-tPA); consider IV labetalol or nicardipine

Initiate anticoagulation with apixaban (preferred over warfarin given prior non-adherence, and preferred DOAC given CKD stage 3a) for secondary stroke prevention, timing 4-14 days post-stroke based on infarct size and hemorrhagic transformation assessment
Order transthoracic echocardiogram to assess cardiac structure and function
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Reduced LVEF 38%, severely dilated LA with spontaneous echo contrast, moderate MR

optimal_actions.action: Admit to stroke unit or neurological ICU for close monitoring with serial neurological checks and telemetry

critical_actions: Establish last known well time to determine thrombolysis eligibility
Obtain emergent non-contrast CT head to exclude hemorrhage before thrombolysis
Confirm INR is not supratherapeutic before administering IV alteplase (patient recently on warfarin)
Administer IV alteplase within the 4.5-hour treatment window
Identify large vessel occlusion and pursue mechanical thrombectomy
Obtain ECG to confirm atrial fibrillation as the stroke mechanism
Initiate anticoagulation for secondary stroke prevention with appropriate DOAC adjusted for renal function
Monitor in ICU/stroke unit with serial neurological assessments
contraindicated_actions: Administering anticoagulation (heparin, DOAC) in the hyperacute phase before excluding hemorrhagic transformation
Aggressively lowering blood pressure below 140/90 in the acute phase (risk of worsening penumbral ischemia)
Administering dual antiplatelet therapy concurrently with IV alteplase within 24 hours
Delaying IV alteplase to obtain MRI when the patient is within the treatment window
Restarting warfarin without bridging plan; a DOAC is preferred given prior non-adherence with warfarin
Using dabigatran for secondary prevention given its high renal clearance in a patient with CKD stage 3a
Performing lumbar puncture in a patient with acute large territory infarction
key_reasoning_points: Sudden onset right hemiparesis and expressive aphasia in a patient with known AF off anticoagulation is a classic cardioembolic stroke presentation
Self-discontinuation of warfarin 3 weeks ago with a now-normalized INR (1.1) explains the loss of stroke prophylaxis and makes the patient eligible for IV alteplase
MRI shows classic acute ischemic stroke pattern: DWI hyperintensity with ADC confirmation in left MCA territory with MRA showing proximal M1 occlusion
Large vessel occlusion mandates mechanical thrombectomy in addition to IV thrombolysis (bridging therapy)
CKD stage 3a must be considered when selecting the anticoagulant for secondary prevention; apixaban is preferred given its lower renal clearance
The patient's known HFrEF with severely dilated LA and spontaneous echo contrast on TTE provides the structural substrate for thrombus formation and cardioembolic stroke
Multiple modifiable risk factors (AF, poorly controlled HTN, poorly controlled DM, HFrEF, hyperlipidemia) must all be addressed in the secondary prevention plan
NIHSS of 16 indicates a severe stroke with significant disability if not treated promptly

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward - classic presentation with sudden onset right hemiparesis and expressive aphasia in a patient with known AF who self-discontinued warfarin, clear LVO on imaging
clinical_pearls: Self-discontinuation of anticoagulation is a common real-world scenario leading to cardioembolic stroke; always inquire about medication adherence
In patients with CKD, apixaban is the preferred DOAC due to its lower dependence on renal clearance (25% renal elimination)
Spontaneous echo contrast ('smoke') in the left atrium on echocardiography indicates blood stasis and markedly elevated thromboembolic risk
The ELAN trial supports earlier initiation of DOACs (within 6-7 days for moderate strokes) compared to traditional 14-day waiting periods
